title: Roth vs Traditional IRA Calculator
description: The math behind the Roth vs Traditional IRA choice often shocks people. We analyzed lifetime tax savings—and the winner isn't what you'd expect.

schemas.headline: Roth vs Traditional IRA Calculator: Which Saves You More in Taxes?
schemas.description: Calculate and compare lifetime tax savings between a Roth IRA and a Traditional IRA with this interactive tool.

mainEntity.name: Should I use a roth vs traditional ira calculator to decide?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, using a calculator helps you visualize the long-term impact of taxes on your investments. It compares your current tax bracket with your expected retirement bracket to determine the optimal strategy.

mainEntity.name: How do I choose between a roth or traditional ira?

acceptedAnswer.text: The choice generally depends on whether you expect your tax rate to be higher now (Traditional) or in retirement (Roth). If you expect to be in a higher tax bracket in retirement, a Roth IRA is usually better. If you expect a lower tax bracket in retirement, a Traditional IRA may be more beneficial.

mainEntity.name: Which ira is better for high income earners?

acceptedAnswer.text: High income earners often benefit from a Traditional IRA because they are currently in a high tax bracket and will likely be in a lower bracket during retirement. However, high income earners may not be eligible to deduct Traditional IRA contributions or contribute directly to a Roth IRA, making a 'Backdoor Roth' strategy popular.

mainEntity.name: What is the Roth IRA contribution limit for 2026?

acceptedAnswer.text: For 2026, the contribution limit for both Roth and Traditional IRAs is expected to be updated by the IRS. Currently, standard limits apply, with catch-up contributions available for those aged 50 and older. Our calculator factors in the standard baseline.

mainEntity.name: Does a Roth IRA have required minimum distributions (RMDs)?

acceptedAnswer.text: No, original owners of a Roth IRA are not required to take minimum distributions (RMDs) during their lifetime, unlike Traditional IRAs which require withdrawals starting at age 73 (or 75 depending on your birth year).
